title: Broadcom Symantec Cloud SWG Threat Events (Broadcom)
dataSources: Symantec WSS access logs with threat fields
spl:
index=proxy sourcetype="symantec:wss:accesslog" earliest=-24h
| where sc_filter_result="DENIED" AND isnotnull(cs_threat_risk_level) AND cs_threat_risk_level IN ("High","Critical")
| stats count by cs_threat, cs_categories, cs_userdn, cs_host
| sort -count
description: Cloud SWG detects malware, phishing, and zero-day threats via URL reputation, content analysis, and sandboxing. Threat events in Splunk enable incident response and threat hunting.
value: Security operations teams analyze Symantec Cloud SWG threat detections, identifying malware and C2 blocks indicating compromised endpoints, finding high-risk traffic that was detected but not blocked (policy gaps), and tracking malware family trends.
implementation: Map `cs_threat` to threat categories. Alert on critical-severity threats. Correlate with endpoint detection for compromised hosts. Report on threat type distribution.
visualization: Bar chart (threats by category), Table (affected users), Timeline (detections), Pie chart (threat types).
cimSpl:
| tstats `summariesonly` count
  from datamodel=Web.Web
  by Web.user, Web.url, Web.http_method span=1h
| sort - count
knownFalsePositives: Anomalies from new SaaS app rollouts, browser updates, or vendor URL category changes.
detailedImplementation:
Prerequisites
• Symantec WSS threat events in `index=symantec` with `sourcetype=symantec:wss`. Key fields: `cs-threat-risk`, `cs-threat-type` (malware/phishing/exploit), `cs-categories` (includes Malware/Phishing categories), `cs-action`, `cs-host`, `cs-userdn`, `cs-malware-name`.
• Symantec WSS threat scanning includes anti-malware (signature + ML), URL reputation, content analysis, and sandboxing. Threat events indicate either successful blocks or detected-but-allowed traffic.

Step 1 — Configure data collection
Verify threat events:
```spl
index=symantec sourcetype="symantec:wss" earliest=-7d
| where tonumber(cs-threat-risk) > 3 OR match(cs-categories, "(?i)malware|phishing")
| stats count by cs-threat-type, cs-action
```

Step 2 — Create the search and alert

Primary search — Threat event dashboard:
```spl
index=symantec sourcetype="symantec:wss" earliest=-24h
| where tonumber(cs-threat-risk) > 5 OR match(cs-categories, "(?i)malware|phishing|command|botnet")
| stats count dc(cs-userdn) as users by cs-threat-type, cs-malware-name, cs-action, cs-host
| sort - count
```

Understanding this SPL: Groups threat events by type, malware name, and action. Events with cs-action=DENIED are successfully blocked threats. Events with cs-action=ALLOWED + high threat risk indicate gaps in the blocking policy — threats detected but not prevented.

Step 3 — Validate
(a) Download EICAR test file. Verify threat detection.
(b) In WSS: Reports > Threats.

Step 4 — Operationalize
Dashboard: Row 1 — Threat type distribution. Row 2 — Allowed high-risk (gaps). Row 3 — Top malware families.
Alert: Immediate on malware/C2 blocks from internal users.

Runbook:
1. **Malware blocked** — Check user endpoint for additional indicators.
2. **High-risk allowed** — Policy gap. Update blocking rules.
3. **C2/Botnet detection** — Internal compromise. Isolate endpoint.

Step 5 — Troubleshooting
• **No threat events** — Threat scanning may be disabled in the WSS policy profile.
• **`cs-malware-name` empty** — Not all threat detections include a malware name (e.g., phishing URLs).
grandmaExplanation: Symantec scans all our web traffic for malware and phishing. We track what it catches and also what it detects but does not block — because detected-but-allowed threats are security gaps we need to close before someone gets infected.
hardware: Symantec Cloud SWG (cloud), Symantec Endpoint Agent
